title: Advanced Calculus
author: Kauffman, Robert M.
subject: calculus
abstract: This course guide has two objectives. First, we cover the major theorems of differential and integral calculus in two semesters, in such a fashion that the students in the class understand what they say and why it is true. Second, successful students will learn how to think logically as mathematicians do, and present the results. We apply these calculus theorems to the study of the elementary functions.

title: Advanced Calculus I
author: Parker, G. Edgar
subject: calculus
abstract: Topics include the logic of quantification, and a theory of sets and ordered pairs. This material is presented with a sequence of problems and theorems, so that students can be given the responsibility for both discovering and understanding why the mathematics covered in this course guide is correct.

title: Advanced Calculus II
author: Parker, G. Edgar
subject: calculus
abstract: This student-oriented course is a continuation of the study of the theory which supports elementary calculus. You will need a copy of the author's Advanced Calculus 1 course guide for reference.

title: Alexander-Wallace-Spanier Cohomology
author: Hildebrant, John A.
subject: topology
abstract: Topics include Abelian groups; cohomology groups; homomorphisms induced by continuous functions; reduced groups in dimension zero; fully normal spaces; inverse limits and the continuity theorem; strong excision theorems, extension, reduction; the map excision theorem; the Mayer-Vietoris theorem; codimension; marginal, peripheral, and inner points; the map addition theorem; acyclicity; and the Vietoris mapping theorem.

title: Better Mathematics Through Matrices
author: Tonne, Phil C.
subject: matrices
abstract: Topics include multiplication of matrices, solving matrix equations, inverses of matrices, commuting matrices, matrix polynomials, complex numbers, matrix similarity, and square roots and cube roots of matrices. The heart of this guide is a series of problems designed to engage your deductive skills, and stimulate your ability to find patterns and make generalizations. The goal is to improve your ability to reason; the vehicle is the study of matrices. Matrices are part of every branch of mathematics, and are a frequent tool in, for example, economics, sociology, and physics -- wherever data is organized.

title: Elementary Mathematics from an Advanced Standpoint
author: Roach, Everett D.

title: First Graduate Course in Differential Equations
